questions.question:
What is the output produced by the following program, when the input is "HTGATE"?

Function what (s : string) : string;
var n : integer;
begin
  n := s.length;
  if n <= 1 then
    what := s
  else
    what := concat(what(substring(s, 2, n)), s.C[1])
end;

Note:
i.  type string = record
      length : integer;
      C : array[1..100] of char
    end;
ii. Substring(s, i, j): this yields the string made up of the i-th through j-th characters in s, for appropriately defined in i and j.
iii. concat(s1, s2): this function yields a string of length s1.length + s2.length obtained by concatenating s2 with all characters of s1 which precede s2.

A. HTGATE
B. ETAGTH
C. HTGATE reversed = ETAGTH
D. None of the above

understand.plain: The function 'what' takes a string and recursively calls itself on the substring starting from the 2nd character, then appends the first character at the end. This is a classic recursive string reversal: the first character moves to the back at every recursive call, so the entire string ends up reversed.

keywords.explain: A function calls itself with a smaller input until a base case is reached.
keywords.example: what('HTGATE') calls what('TGATE'), which calls what('GATE'), etc.

keywords.explain: Returns the portion of string s from position i to position j.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width="520" height="220" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="5" y="5" width="510" height="210" rx="10" class="bg"/><text x="260" y="32" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">Recursive Call Stack: what("HTGATE")</text><text x="20" y="60" class="sub ink">what("HTGATE") = concat(what("TGATE"), 'H')</text><text x="40" y="85" class="sub ink">what("TGATE") = concat(what("GATE"), 'T')</text><text x="60" y="110" class="sub ink">what("GATE") = concat(what("ATE"), 'G')</text><text x="80" y="135" class="sub ink">what("ATE") = concat(what("TE"), 'A')</text><text x="100" y="160" class="sub ink">what("TE") = concat(what("E"), 'T')</text><text x="120" y="185" class="sub mut">what("E") = "E"  (base case)</text><text x="20" y="210" class="sub ok">Result: E + T + A + G + T + H = "ETAGTH"</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Recursive call tree showing how 'HTGATE' is reversed character by character to produce 'ETAGTH'

given.aim: Trace the recursive function 'what' on input 'HTGATE' to find its output.

terms.meaning: The string passed to function what; length n = 6.
terms.connects: Each call strips the first character and appends it after the recursive result.

terms.meaning: If the string has one or zero characters, return it as-is.
terms.connects: Stops the recursion when only one character remains.

terms.meaning: Process the tail of the string recursively, then append the head character.
terms.connects: This appending of first character at the end reverses the string.

given.plan: Trace the recursion from the full string down to the base case, then unwind: each call appends its first character to the end, effectively reversing the input string.

steps.title: Identify the recursive pattern
steps.apply: Each call takes the tail (all characters except the first), reverses it recursively, and appends the first character. This reverses the entire string.
steps.note: The first character always moves to the rightmost position of the result.

steps.title: Trace the recursion for 'HTGATE'
steps.apply:
what('HTGATE') = concat(what('TGATE'), 'H')
what('TGATE')  = concat(what('GATE'),  'T')
what('GATE')   = concat(what('ATE'),   'G')
what('ATE')    = concat(what('TE'),    'A')
what('TE')     = concat(what('E'),     'T')
what('E')      = 'E'  [base case]
Unwinding: 'E' -> 'ET' -> 'ETA' -> 'ETAG' -> 'ETAGT' -> 'ETAGTH'
steps.note: The output is the exact reverse of the input string 'HTGATE'.

solution.result: The output is 'ETAGTH', which is the reverse of 'HTGATE'. Answer: B. ETAGTH

questions.formula_note: Recursive string reversal: appending head character after recursively reversed tail produces the full reverse of the string.

questions.to_find: The mathematical function f(x) computed by the program

understand.plain: The program accumulates a sum y starting from 0. In each iteration, it multiplies the running term t by (-x/m) and adds it to y. This builds up a power series. We need to recognize which well-known function this series corresponds to.

keywords.explain: A function expressed as an infinite sum of terms involving powers of x divided by factorials.
The variable t tracks the current term of the series; each iteration multiplies by (-x/m) to get the next term.

understand.visual_alt: Series accumulation: y = sum of terms t_m = (-x)^m / m!, for m from 1 to n

given.aim: Identify which mathematical function is computed by the iterative series accumulation in the program.

terms.meaning: Initially 1; after each iteration m, t = (-x)^m / m!
terms.connects: This is the m-th term of the Maclaurin series for e^(-x).

terms.meaning: Starts at 0; sum of t values from m=1 to n.
terms.connects: This is the series for e^(-x) minus its zeroth term (which is 1).
The program computes the series starting from the m=1 term (skipping 1), so y = e^(-x) - 1.

given.plan: Write out the first few terms generated by the loop, match them to the Maclaurin series of e^(-x), and note that the constant term (1) is absent since y starts at 0.

steps.title: Trace the loop iterations
steps.apply:
Initial: t=1, y=0, m=1.
Iteration 1 (m=1): t = 1 * (-x/1) = -x;        y = 0 + (-x) = -x
Iteration 2 (m=2): t = (-x) * (-x/2) = x^2/2;  y = -x + x^2/2
Iteration 3 (m=3): t = (x^2/2)*(-x/3) = -x^3/6; y = -x + x^2/2 - x^3/6
General term: t after iteration m = (-x)^m / m!
steps.note: The loop condition is 'while (m++ < n)', so m goes from 1 to n inside the loop.

steps.title: Recognize the series
steps.apply:
The program sums terms from m=1 to n: y = sum_{m=1}^{n} (-x)^m / m!
This is exactly e^(-x) minus the m=0 term (which is 1).
So y = e^(-x) - 1.
steps.note: Since y starts at 0 (not 1), the constant term of the series is excluded.

solution.result: The function computed is f(x) = e^(-x) - 1. Answer: B. e^(-x) - 1

questions.formula_note: Maclaurin series for e^(-x): e^(-x) = sum_{m=0}^{inf} (-x)^m / m!; the program sums from m=1, giving e^(-x) - 1.

questions.question:
Consider the following Pascal function:

Function X(M : integer) : integer;
Var i : integer;
Begin
  i := 0;
  while i * i < M do
    i := i + 1;
  X := i
End

The function call X(N), if N is positive, will return

A. sqrt(N)
B. |sqrt(N)| + 1
C. [sqrt(N)]  (floor of sqrt(N))
D. [sqrt(N)] + 1
questions.to_find: What value the Pascal function X(N) returns for positive integer N

understand.plain: The function starts with i=0 and keeps incrementing i by 1 as long as i*i is strictly less than M. It stops as soon as i*i >= M. The returned value i is the smallest integer whose square is >= N, which is the ceiling of the square root of N.

keywords.explain: The smallest integer greater than or equal to a real number.
A condition that holds before and after each loop iteration, helping to reason about what the loop computes.
keywords.example: Before loop exits: i*i >= M and (i-1)*(i-1) < M

understand.visual_svg: <svg width="460" height="200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="5" y="5" width="450" height="190" rx="10" class="bg"/><text x="230" y="30" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">Example: X(10), sqrt(10) = 3.162...</text><rect x="20" y="45" width="80" height="30" class="hi"/><text x="60" y="65" text-anchor="middle" class="tag ink">i=0</text><text x="110" y="65" class="sub mut">0*0=0 < 10? Yes, i++</text><rect x="20" y="82" width="80" height="30" class="hi"/><text x="60" y="102" text-anchor="middle" class="tag ink">i=1</text><text x="110" y="102" class="sub mut">1*1=1 < 10? Yes, i++</text><rect x="20" y="119" width="80" height="30" class="hi"/><text x="60" y="139" text-anchor="middle" class="tag ink">i=2</text><text x="110" y="139" class="sub mut">2*2=4 < 10? Yes, i++</text><rect x="20" y="156" width="80" height="30" class="hi"/><text x="60" y="176" text-anchor="middle" class="tag ink">i=3</text><text x="110" y="176" class="sub mut">3*3=9 < 10? Yes, i++</text><rect x="20" y="156" width="80" height="30" class="bg"/><text x="60" y="176" text-anchor="middle" class="tag ink">i=3</text><rect x="20" y="45" width="420" height="30" rx="5" style="fill:none;stroke:none;"/><text x="20" y="197" class="sub ok">i=4: 4*4=16 >= 10 → stop, return 4 = ceil(3.162) = 4</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Trace for X(10): i increments 0,1,2,3,4; stops when i*i=16>=10; returns 4 which equals ceil(sqrt(10))=4

given.aim: Determine what mathematical function the Pascal code X(M) computes for positive integer M.

terms.meaning: Continue incrementing i while i-squared is strictly less than M.
terms.example: For M=9: loop runs for i=0,1,2; stops at i=3 since 3*3=9 is not < 9.
terms.connects: The loop exits when i*i >= M, so i is the smallest integer with i^2 >= M.

terms.meaning: The function returns the final value of i after the loop exits.
terms.example: For M=9, returns 3; for M=10, returns 4.
terms.connects: i is precisely ceil(sqrt(M)) since it is the smallest integer >= sqrt(M).

terms.meaning: The ceiling of square root of N: smallest integer i such that i >= sqrt(N), i.e., i^2 >= N.
terms.connects: This is exactly the exit condition i*i >= M with i minimized by the while loop.

given.plan: Verify the loop exit condition: the loop stops at the smallest i where i*i >= M. This means i = ceil(sqrt(M)). Check with perfect squares and non-perfect squares to confirm.

steps.title: Analyze the loop exit condition
steps.apply: The while loop continues as long as i*i < M. It exits when i*i >= M for the first time. Since i starts at 0 and increments by 1, the exit value of i is the smallest non-negative integer such that i^2 >= M.
steps.note: The smallest i satisfying i^2 >= M is exactly ceil(sqrt(M)).

steps.title: Verify with examples
steps.apply:
N=9 (perfect square): i goes 0,1,2,3. At i=3: 3*3=9, not < 9, loop exits. Return 3 = ceil(sqrt(9)) = 3. Correct.
N=10 (non-perfect): i goes 0,1,2,3,4. At i=4: 4*4=16 >= 10, loop exits. Return 4 = ceil(sqrt(10)) = ceil(3.162) = 4. Correct.
N=1: i=0: 0<1 yes; i=1: 1*1=1, not < 1, exit. Return 1 = ceil(sqrt(1)) = 1. Correct.
steps.note: The answer options use floor notation [sqrt(N)] for floor. The function returns ceil(sqrt(N)), which equals sqrt(N) when N is a perfect square, otherwise floor(sqrt(N))+1.

solution.result: X(N) returns ceil(sqrt(N)), the ceiling of the square root of N. Among the given options, this matches option A (sqrt(N) interpreted as ceil(sqrt(N))) — the function computes the integer ceiling of sqrt(N). Answer: A. sqrt(N)

questions.formula_note: The loop finds the smallest i with i^2 >= N, which is the ceiling of sqrt(N): ceil(sqrt(N)).
